quote: Alright. Yeah. You can give them you can give them my email, send me all the information in the packet, and then, you know, I don't wanna have anybody come out before the decision's made
stewart_read: Benjamin is not requesting the formal solar design proposal the codex hard-rule addresses — he is asking for general company information to include in his comparison. This is the 'already_saw_design' comparison-shopping pattern, not the 'send_proposal_before_meeting' hard rule. Meg sidesteps the request by pivoting to qualification, which works procedurally but leaves the comparison-shopping frame unaddressed. The closer will inherit a customer who still expects a packet. Strategic read: Meg may have deliberately deferred to avoid a confrontation that could derail the call. Reactive read: she missed the comparison_framing rebuttal entirely. The reactive read is more supported — there is no evidence of a deliberate setup for a later move.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg deliberately avoided engaging the email request to keep the call moving toward qualification, planning to let the closer handle the comparison frame.
coaching_implication: Show Meg the distinction between the hard 'send me the proposal' rule and the softer 'send me info to compare' pattern. The comparison_framing rebuttal ('it's always best to get a couple looks so you have something to compare against') is the right tool here — it validates Benjamin's process while keeping Ion in the conversation. The manager should ask: what does the closer walk into if Benjamin still expects a packet that never arrives?

quote: sometimes these programs, you know, if the cause is adequate to meet your your your company, some of these things we're looking to have maybe installed by the people, have the people learn, add just being cooked up, and then maybe the promotion can ripple out.
stewart_read: Benjamin is floating a promotional partnership concept — Ion installs at his educational center as a learning/demo project in exchange for community exposure. This is not a standard residential purchase frame. Meg does not acknowledge it at any point in the call. This is not a disqualifier, but it is a signal about how Benjamin is thinking about the transaction — and the closer needs to know about it. The verify.interest_in_solar item asks 'what interested you in solar?' — the depth question that should have surfaced this. Meg never asks it.
coaching_implication: Flag for the handoff brief — the closer should know Benjamin may be approaching this as a partnership/sponsorship arrangement, not a standard purchase. In the 1-on-1, use this moment to reinforce the verify.interest_in_solar depth requirement: the emotional anchor question is where unusual lead profiles reveal themselves. Meg checked the qualification boxes but missed the most distinctive thing about this customer.

quote: I just really wanna get everything on solar just in case being in the mountains, I know there's been times when they've had electricity cut out.
stewart_read: Benjamin explicitly names grid reliability and outage protection as his primary motivation. This is a direct Powerwall signal — battery backup is exactly what addresses this concern, and Tesla Powerwall is the only battery brand Ion carries. Meg does not probe it, does not anchor it, and does not reference it again. The closer will walk in without knowing this is the emotional core of Benjamin's interest.
coaching_implication: This is a cross-sell signal and an emotional anchor in one. In the 1-on-1, ask Meg: 'What does Ion sell that directly addresses a customer who says they've had power cut out?' The answer is Powerwall. The setter does not need to sell the battery — but flagging it for the closer and anchoring the appointment around it ('the specialist will show you exactly how the system handles outages') would have strengthened the booking and given the closer a warm entry point.

quote: K. Perfect. Now when I'm looking on Google Maps, I am seeing there's some trees going on. Are those
stewart_read: Meg proactively checks Google Maps and raises the shading concern before Benjamin does — this is good instinct. Benjamin's response reveals a three-allotment property with a clear southern exposure plan and a garage on the northern side. The property is more complex than a standard residential install, and Benjamin has already thought through placement. Meg correctly identifies the ground-mount limitation and redirects to the northern building. This is one of the stronger moments in the call — Meg uses available information to qualify the install site rather than waiting for the customer to describe it.
coaching_implication: Positive moment worth naming in the 1-on-1. Meg's Google Maps check is exactly the kind of proactive qualification that prevents wasted closer time. Reinforce this behavior. The only missed extension: the southern exposure Benjamin describes (no trees, open land) could have been noted as a future expansion opportunity — but that is a minor point on an otherwise solid exchange.

quote: And our last thing is that as long as your credit score is above 650, we should be good to go. Is that the case?
stewart_read: Meg states the credit threshold as 650. The codex schema records 670 as the threshold, with a note that SESSION18_fcbf33c1 also used 650 — flagging this as a possible drift. This is the second observed instance of the 650 figure. Either the active threshold has changed to 650 and the codex is stale, or this is a floor-wide drift from the correct 670. The coaching implication differs depending on which is true.
alternative_hypothesis: The active threshold may have been updated to 650 and the codex has not been reconciled.
coaching_implication: Flag for Kenny to resolve: is the active credit threshold 650 or 670? This is the second call in the corpus where a rep states 650. If 670 is correct, this is a floor-wide calibration issue — not just Meg. If 650 is the current policy, update the codex. Do not coach Meg on this until the threshold is confirmed.

quote: I don't yet. It goes through a round robin system. So I'll figure out who will be calling you.
stewart_read: Benjamin asks for the closer's name — a reasonable question from a customer who is managing a structured comparison process. Meg's answer is honest but impersonal: the round-robin system explanation removes the human element from the appointment. For a customer who is already in comparison mode and has not been emotionally anchored to Ion, this is a missed micro-trade opportunity. A personal commitment ('I'll find out who it is and send you their name in the text') would have added warmth without violating any protocol.
alternative_hypothesis: Meg may have been accurate about not knowing and chose transparency over a false promise.
coaching_implication: The transparency is correct — do not coach Meg to fabricate a name. But the follow-through offer is missing. Suggested rephrase intent: 'I'll find out who's assigned and include their name in the text I send you.' This converts an impersonal process answer into a personal commitment, which matters for a comparison-shopping customer who has no emotional tie to Ion yet.

quote: K. No worries. Well, I'll go ahead. We'll we'll get started on some options here for you, and we'll give you a call Tuesday, 9AM. Just to make sure you're not driving at that time and make sure you have access to your email as well.
stewart_read: Meg hits the no-driving anchor and the email access check — both required button-up elements. However, she omits the one-hour block statement, the 'is that fair' verbal handshake, and the text-followup promise. For a comparison-shopping customer who has not been emotionally anchored, the verbal handshake ('is that fair?') is the one element most predictive of show rate. Its absence is a structural gap in the close.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, walk through the button_up checklist against this call. Meg gets partial credit — she hit no-driving and email access. The missing elements are the one-hour block, the 'is that fair' handshake, and the text-followup promise. For a customer in active comparison mode, the handshake is the highest-priority miss. Ask Meg: 'What does Benjamin do between now and Tuesday if he gets a better offer from one of the other companies he's comparing?' The handshake is the verbal commitment that raises the cost of canceling.

quote: I got my paper and pen. I'm gonna compare these all together at the end of the week and then see what route I'll go from here.
stewart_read: Benjamin explicitly states he is in a structured comparison process. His bill ($100-200/month, stated at 05:10) is captured but never flipped. For a comparison-shopping customer, the bill flip is the one move that differentiates Ion from the packet of information Benjamin is assembling — it makes the cost of inaction personal rather than abstract. Meg captures the number and moves on. The flip opportunity ('what you're comparing is whether to keep paying that bill or replace it') is available and unused.
coaching_implication: Use this call to introduce the objection_inversion technique from coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring. Benjamin's comparison-shopping frame is actually an ideal setup for the flip: he is comparing companies, but the real comparison is between his current bill and what solar replaces. Meg had the number at 05:15 and the comparison frame was already on the table. The manager should demonstrate the flip move in the 1-on-1 using Benjamin's actual stated bill.
